feed.title: blank
feed.subtitle: A personal website of Jun Yamamoto.

entry.title: DYNASNET Retreat Workshop in Visegrad
entry.content: I participated in the DYNASNET Retreat workshop which took place in Visegrad on 5th-8th February, 2024.
entry.summary: I participated in the DYNASNET Retreat workshop which took place in Visegrad on 5th-8th February, 2024.

entry.title: Data Stories 2023
entry.content: I participated in Data Stories 2023 which took place at Central European University on 20th November 2023. I submitted my poster titled “A Physical Network of Rivers” in which I visualised the physical and topological networks of Tone River in Kanto, Japan. You can find my poster here.
entry.summary: I participated in Data Stories 2023 which took place at Central European University on 20th November 2023. I submitted my poster titled “A Physical Network of Rivers” in which I visualised the physical and topological networks of Tone River in Kanto, Japan. You can find my poster here.

entry.title: A paper is published in Physical Review E.

link.title: A paper is published in Physical Review E.

entry.content:
My paper on the bifractality of fractal scale-free networks (FSFNs) is published from Physical Review E.

In this work, we investigated the structures of scale-free networks with fractal property (in which the network diameter follows a power law w.r.t. the number of nodes). In contrast to the small-world networks, fractal networks have large network diameters (or average shortest path lengths) and are typically disassortative in terms of degrees and characterized by the scale invariance, i.e., network properties are preserved under scale transformations. We showed that many FSFNs are structurally bifractal, characterized by two distinct local fractal dimensions; the smaller one corresponding to regions containing the inifinite-degree hub nodes and the larger one corresponding to regions with finite-degree nodes that are infinitely far from the infinite hubs. From our findings, we conjecture that any FSFN is bifractal. This implies that dynamical processes on FSFNs may be influenced by the distinct local fractalities depending on the regions in which the process takes place.

The article can be found here or on arXiv.
entry.summary: My paper on the bifractality of fractal scale-free networks (FSFNs) is published from Physical Review E.

entry.title: NetSci2023@Vienna
entry.content: I will participate in NetSci2023 at Vienna, Austria from 11th - 14th July, 2023.
entry.summary: I will participate in NetSci2023 at Vienna, Austria from 11th - 14th July, 2023.

entry.title: Starting a PhD in Network Science at Central European University

link.title: Starting a PhD in Network Science at Central European University

entry.content: I am thrilled to announce that I will pursue a PhD in Network Science at the Department of Network and Data Science (DNDS) at Central European University (CEU) from September 2023. I am trully honoured to be given a fully-funded offer from CEU, and I am looking forward to joining DNDS.
entry.summary: I am thrilled to announce that I will pursue a PhD in Network Science at the Department of Network and Data Science (DNDS) at Central European University (CEU) from September 2023. I am trully honoured to be given a fully-funded offer from CEU, and I am looking forward to joining DNDS.

entry.title: Network Science Seminar 2022@Doshisha University
entry.content: I participated in the network science seminar at Doushisha University (link) in Kyoto, Japan on August 23-25, 2022 and gave a poster presentation about my work on the bifractal nature of fractal scale-free networks and its implications. I would like to express my gratitude to Dr. Shogo Mizutaka for funding my visit.
entry.summary: The Network Science Seminar 2022 @ Doshisha University.

entry.title: Visit to Hasegawa Lab@Ibaraki University
entry.content:
I visited Hasegawa Lab at Ibaraki University from 13 to 15 July, 2022.
I would like to thank Prof. Takehisa Hasegawa for inviting me and providing me with a lot of insights/advices to my research (and career).
entry.summary: A visit to Hasegawa Lab@Ibaraki University

entry.title: My last day at Condensed Matter Physics Lab@Hokkaido University

link.title: My last day at Condensed Matter Physics Lab@Hokkaido University

entry.content:
Today was my last day at the Laboratory of Condensed Matter Physics at Hokkaido University. The last two years at the lab have motivated me to further my interests in complex networks. I express my sincere gratitude to my supervisor, Prof. Kousuke Yakubo, for his mentorship during my study.

I will attend Network Science Seminar 2022 at Doushisha University next week (Aug. 23-25, 2022) and then depart for London to start my study for an MSc in Mathematics at the Queen Mary University of London.

I wish every member of the lab good luck, and I hope to enjoy my new journey at QMUL.
entry.summary: My last day at the Laboratory of Condensed Matter Physics@Hokkaido University

entry.title: Network Science Workshop@JAIST
entry.content: I participated in the network science workshop at Japan Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (JAIST) on May 20-21, 2022 and gave a one-hour oral presentation about my work on the structural bifractality of fractal scale-free networks. I would like to express my gratitude to Dr. Shogo Mizutaka and Dr. Yosuke Murase for organizing the event and inviting me.
entry.summary: The network science workship at JAIST
